mnemonics.title: Hash Properties - PRE
mnemonics.content: P-RE: Pre-image Resistance, Second Pre-image Resistance, Collision resistance - All 'Resistance' mean hard for attacker to break!

mnemonics.title: MD5 vs SHA Memory
mnemonics.content:
MD5 = 128 bits (12 letters = 12*10+8=128) - BROKEN
SHA-1 = 160 bits - BROKEN
SHA-256 = 256 bits - SECURE
Number tells you the bit length!

mnemonics.title: HMAC Structure
mnemonics.content:
HMAC = H(K⊕opad || H(K⊕ipad || M))
'OUTER' uses 'O'pad, 'INNER' uses 'I'pad
Think: O-pad = Outer, I-pad = Inner

acronyms.usage: Provides integrity and authentication using hash functions with secret key
Small cryptographic tag verifying message integrity and authenticity
MAC using block ciphers like AES instead of hash functions
Family of cryptographic hash functions by NIST (SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-3)
Family of hash functions (MD4, MD5) producing message digests

visualTips.visualization: Think of hash as a unique digital fingerprint - each person (message) has a unique fingerprint (hash). Impossible to recreate person from fingerprint alone.
Imagine 23 people in room - 50% chance two share birthday. Similarly, with 2^(n/2) attempts, collision becomes likely in n-bit hash. Draw probability curve!
Draw two boxes - inner box (key+ipad+message) hashed, then outer box (result+opad+key) hashed again. Like onion layers protecting the message!
Think of salt as adding unique seasoning to each password. Even same password gets different 'taste' (hash) because of unique salt.

analogies.analogy: Like a blender - easy to blend fruits into juice (hash), but impossible to unblend juice back into original fruits (irreversible).
Hash = seal on letter (shows if tampered). MAC = seal + registered mail (shows if tampered AND proves who sent it).
Like two different keys opening same lock - bad for security! Good hash functions prevent this.
Like recognizing a famous person's face (hash), but impossible to recreate their exact appearance (original message).
